title: Add the first derived governance-facing main wall using the shared tags
description:
add the first real governance-facing main wall for the operator, starting with the currently active visible projects and using the shared tags while keeping the full task ledger separate and available.

Scope In:
derived main wall section, compact cards, tagged signals, Program Kanban plus TacticsGame as first visible project sources, preservation of the full canonical board as a separate detailed view, read-only validation.

Scope Out:
editing, generic markdown browsing, broad workspace-wide aggregation beyond the first visible projects, persistence, file watching.

Acceptance Criteria:
- the governance-facing main wall shows `Project`, `ID`, `Title`, `Status`, `Layer`, `Category`, `Owner`, and `Review State` by default
- the first wall includes at least `Program Kanban` and `TacticsGame`
- the full task ledger remains available separately
- `Review` and `Blocked` items are visually prominent
- the dashboard remains read-only
- the current project snapshot remains reconstructable from markdown alone
result_summary: Implemented as the first real read-only main wall. The app now derives the five approved columns from `Program Kanban/KANBAN.md` and `TacticsGame/KANBAN.md`, applies compact default tag cards with restrained yellow and blue project accents, and keeps `Recently Completed` strict to explicit `Completed At` timestamps within the last 48 hours. The operator has now approved closure after live review.

result_summary: Implemented as a separate read-only `Milestones` mode alongside the existing main wall. The server reads both project `MILESTONES.md` files, derives per-project milestone cards plus linked task lists from markdown, and shows simple milestone progress counts without weakening markdown source-of-truth rules. Current milestone panels expand by default, and the first slice stays intentionally lightweight rather than turning into a KPI-heavy reporting system. After the first live review, milestone ordering was corrected so cards preserve numeric order (`1`, `2`, `3`) instead of surfacing the current milestone first. The operator has now approved the result and closed the task.

title: Design milestone-view project filtering with support for up to two visible projects at once
description:
define the safest first milestone-view filtering model so the operator can choose up to two projects at a time without turning the milestone surface into a heavy dashboard configuration tool.

Scope In:
filter interaction model, default last-used selection state, one-project minimum and two-project maximum behavior, chip-based interaction consistent with the main wall, local-storage persistence, future-scale guardrails, read-only derived filtering, and any needed simplification recommendations.

Scope Out:
implementation, saved preferences, arbitrary filter-builder logic, search/reporting expansion, editing, cloud sync.

Acceptance Criteria:
- a bounded milestone-filter model exists for one-to-two visible projects
- the design clarifies the default state and how multi-selection behaves
- the filter style stays visually similar to the main-wall chip treatment
- selection persists between restarts in local storage
- if a third project is selected later, the UI blocks it and shows a small message
- when fewer than the maximum number of projects are selected, the layout remains stable rather than reflowing into a different card geometry
- if only one project is selected, it appears in the left slot and the right slot remains blank
- the underlying model allows any two projects to be selected together
- for the current phase, `Program Kanban` remains the default left-side project in the preselection state
- the design anticipates future project growth without overengineering the first slice
- the output is clear enough for a later implementation task
result_summary: Opened after operator review requested milestone-view filtering but explicitly allowed the first proposed behavior to stay simple and bounded. Current design direction: use chips similar to the main wall, persist last-used selection, block any third project with a lightweight message, and keep the two-panel layout stable rather than dynamically reflowing widths. The structural model should allow any two projects to be selected together, but the current preselection should still default to `Program Kanban` on the left. If only one project is selected, it should occupy the left slot and leave the right side blank rather than inventing a fake empty card.

title: Design lightweight click-to-answer response chips for operator question flows
description:
explore whether Program Kanban should support lightweight click-to-answer response chips so the operator can respond to common clarification questions without always typing or copy-pasting, while preserving freeform responses as an option.

Scope In:
interaction concept, recommended use cases, when chips are appropriate versus not, fallback to manual typing, relation to approvals and design questions, and bounded read-only or low-risk input expectations.

Scope Out:
implementation, full chat replacement, workflow automation, open-ended form builders, broad conversational UI redesign.

Acceptance Criteria:
- a bounded concept exists for optional response chips
- the design preserves manual/freeform responses as a first-class fallback
- the concept is clear about which question types are safe for chip-based answering
- the first safe use cases are bounded yes/no confirmations, single-choice recommendation questions, and short design-choice questions
- chips appear only when the orchestrator explicitly presents options, not as a broad automatic replacement for normal conversation
- the first version is optimized for clarity over speed
- the first version is single-select only
- every chip set includes a clear path to type a custom answer instead
- chip clicks only stage local selections; they do not submit immediately
- a multi-question set uses one shared `Submit answers` action for the whole set
- chip answers are stored only as normal conversation or task decisions after submission, not as ambient persistent preferences
- the output is clear enough for a later implementation task if the operator wants to pursue it
result_summary: Opened after operator feedback that recommendation-based question flows would be easier if common answers could be selected directly instead of always typed manually. Current design direction: chips should appear only when explicitly presented, support only single-select bounded answers first, always preserve a custom typed-response path, prioritize clarity over speed, and queue local selections until the operator uses one shared `Submit answers` action for the whole set.

title: Add source-of-truth consistency checks between task details, board columns, and milestone-derived views
description:
harden Program Kanban against drift by adding a bounded consistency-checking path that can detect mismatches between kanban column placement, task-detail status fields, and milestone-derived snapshot output, starting with status mismatch detection across both visible projects.

Scope In:
drift-detection design, first-pass status mismatch detection, milestone-parser safety checks as a later extension, snapshot verification boundary, local-only validation path, debug-surface direction, and explicit non-goals for broader automation.

Scope Out:
editing from the dashboard, cloud sync, generic markdown linting, background workflow automation, replacing markdown as source of truth.

Acceptance Criteria:
- the design defines how to detect board-column versus task-detail status mismatches
- the first pass is explicitly scoped to both visible projects
- the first slice only checks these mismatch types:
- task is shown in one board column but task-detail `Status` maps to another
- task is `Done` but `Review State` is not `Accepted`
- task has `Review State: Accepted` but is not `Done`
- the first slice leaves milestone-parser and fake-card checks for later follow-through
- mismatch checks run on snapshot generation and on manual refresh through the same lightweight path
- the design defines where mismatch warnings appear in both debug output and a lightweight UI surface
- the lightweight UI surface is hidden behind a debug toggle near refresh
- the default UI behavior shows a small non-blocking warning badge or count rather than interrupting normal use
- the first visible mismatch detail is lightweight: task ID, project, and a short mismatch reason
- mismatch warnings are grouped by project
- `Completed At` validation is out of scope for the first pass except where later `Done`-state hardening may require it
- milestone-heading or fake-card detection is preserved as a follow-up concern rather than silently mixed into the first status-only slice
- the solution stays compatible with the read-only markdown-derived architecture
- the output is clear enough to guide a later implementation slice without widening into a generic workflow engine
result_summary: Implemented as a bounded first-pass consistency check in the snapshot path and manual refresh path. The UI now exposes a non-blocking mismatch count near refresh plus grouped mismatch details behind a debug toggle. First-pass checks cover the three approved status/review mismatch types only. Follow-up correction applied after operator review: the detector now treats legacy `Done` items without explicit task-detail blocks as already closed legacy work rather than fresh review-state drift, while still surfacing real active-source mismatches such as true board/detail status disagreement. The operator has now validated the detector behavior and accepted closure.

title: Add a small project-filter area above the main-wall columns
description:
add a lightweight project-filter control above the main wall so the operator can intentionally choose which two visible projects are shown without changing markdown source-of-truth rules.

Scope In:
small top-of-wall filter area, toggle-chip interaction, one-to-two visible project behavior for the current phase, last-used selection persistence, read-only derived filtering, stable-column behavior, and local visual verification.

Scope Out:
milestone-view filtering, arbitrary custom filters, single-project-only mode, editing, cross-project permission logic.

Acceptance Criteria:
- the main wall exposes a small visible filter area above the columns
- each visible project has a simple toggle-chip control
- the main wall supports one or two active projects
- any two projects can be active together for the current phase
- last-used selection persists between restarts
- columns remain structurally stable while project content changes
- if the operator tries to deselect the last active project, the UI blocks it and shows a small message
- if a third project is selected, the UI blocks it with a small message
- filtering remains derived-view behavior only and does not alter markdown state
result_summary: Implemented as a small chip row above the wall. The filter now matches the milestone-view model more closely: one or two projects can be active at once, selection persists locally, and the wall columns remain stable while the visible cards change. The live workspace now includes `AI Capability Training` as the third structural project for real filter testing. After operator clarification, the wall no longer pins `Program Kanban`; any one or two projects can now be active. The operator has now validated the behavior and accepted closure.

title: Redefine the Program Kanban board columns and gate rules for the new wall
description:
Rework the Program Kanban board to use the operator-approved workflow columns and explicit entry criteria.

Requested column model:
- Backlog = ideas and tasks with incomplete requirements
- Ready for Build = all requirements captured and no blockers
- In Progress = being built or corrected
- In Review = tasks waiting operator review
- Complete = reviewed and closed

Open requirement gaps to resolve before build:
- whether Blocked and Deferred remain visible as secondary columns or become badges/states inside the five-column model
- whether every task must belong to a milestone before it can leave Backlog
- what exact gates move a task from Backlog to Ready for Build
- how legacy imported tasks should map into the revised column model without losing traceability
